coredata.dc:title: Incorporation of cadmium sulfide quantum dots in photoactive layer of quaternary organic solar cell

coredata.dc:creator: Issa, Nour Attallah
Tan, Sin Tee
Chia, Chin Hua
Lau, Kam Sheng
Hj Jumali, Mohammad Hafizuddin
Chong, Kok-Keong
Yap, Chi Chin
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs have previously been used to enhance the photovoltaic performance of ternary organic solar cells (OSCs). Different from the previous reports, both CQDs and CdS QDs synthesized via the wet chemical route were incorporated into the photoactive layers of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):(6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) to construct quaternary OSCs in this work. The effects of incorporating both CQDs and CdS QDs in photoactive layers on the photovoltaic performance of OSCs were investigated in detail. The structure of the OSCs was fluorine tin oxide (FTO)/ZnO/photoactive layer/Ag. The ZnO electron transport layer in nanorod arrays form was prepared using hydrothermal method, whereas the photoactive layer was deposited using spin coating technique. The OSCs with CdS QDs demonstrated a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 2.87 %, an increment of 12 % in comparison to that without CdS QDs. The introduction of CdS QDs not only improves light absorption but also increases the exciton dissociation and electron transport.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Electron transport
Exciton dissociation
Organic photovoltaic
Wet chemical route

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: J−V characteristics of OSCs with and without CdS QDs under AM 1.5G 100 mW/cm2 illumination.
FESEM cross-sectional images of (a) P3HT:PC61BM:CQDs and (b) P3HT:PC61BM:CQDs:CdS QDs deposited on ZnO nanorod arrays.
(a) The UV–Vis absorption spectra and (b) normalized absorption spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs, (c) UV–Vis absorption of CdS QDs.
Raman spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs.
(a) The PL spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs and (b) energy level diagram of different combinations of donor-acceptors.
IPCE spectra of OSCs with and without CdS QDs.
Photovoltaic parameters of OSCs with and without CdS QDs.
Photovoltaic parameters of OSCs with different concentrations of CdS QDs.

head.ce:title: Incorporation of cadmium sulfide quantum dots in photoactive layer of quaternary organic solar cell

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs have previously been used to enhance the photovoltaic performance of ternary organic solar cells (OSCs). Different from the previous reports, both CQDs and CdS QDs synthesized via the wet chemical route were incorporated into the photoactive layers of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):(6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) to construct quaternary OSCs in this work. The effects of incorporating both CQDs and CdS QDs in photoactive layers on the photovoltaic performance of OSCs were investigated in detail. The structure of the OSCs was fluorine tin oxide (FTO)/ZnO/photoactive layer/Ag. The ZnO electron transport layer in nanorod arrays form was prepared using hydrothermal method, whereas the photoactive layer was deposited using spin coating technique. The OSCs with CdS QDs demonstrated a power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 2.87 %, an increment of 12 % in comparison to that without CdS QDs. The introduction of CdS QDs not only improves light absorption but also increases the exciton dissociation and electron transport.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar energy is one of the most effective ways of solving the world's energy issue. An organic solar cell (OSC) that utilizes an organic molecule and a conjugated polymer shows excellent potential as a cost-effective and easily processed alternative to traditional inorganic solar cells. It is lightweight, flexible, and has a sufficiently high power conversion efficiency (PCE), making it particularly suitable for indoor light applications [1–5]. Furthermore, OSC materials exhibit chemical customizability, tunable optoelectronic properties, and high mechanical flexibility [6]. Incorporating a third component into the photoactive layer has been shown to be a viable approach in enhancing various photovoltaic parameters and the overall performance of ternary OSCs [7–13].

Due to their exceptional optical and electrical properties, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with various concentrations have been added to the photoactive layer of poly(3-hexylthiophene) (P3HT):(6,6)-phenyl-C61-butyric acid methyl ester (PC61BM) [14]. The OSC with 40 vt% CQDs showed nearly 37 % increment in PCE with respect to those without CQDs. The enhanced light absorption, exciton dissociation, and charge transport contribute to the considerable boost in photovoltaic performance. Besides, the addition of cadmium sulfide (CdS) QDs to the photoactive layer of P3HT and PC61BM also results in a significant 25 % improvement in PCE compared to the control device without CdS QDs. The enhancement of photovoltaic performance is primarily due to the increase in short-circuit current density (Jsc) resulting from improved absorption in the wavelength range of 350–550 nm [15]. In addition, a more significant improvement of PCE (approximately 50 %) has been achieved by incorporating CdS QDs into photoactive layer because of improved light absorption and increased exciton dissociation [16].

Quaternary OSC is an advanced form of organic photovoltaic technology in which the photoactive layer consists of four different components, unlike single-junction or binary OSCs, which only use one donor and one acceptor. These materials usually consist of combinations of substances that act as donors and acceptors. Quaternary OSCs based on P3HT:PC61BM have been shown in numerous studies to outperform their ternary and binary counterparts in terms of PCE [17–19] In view of the advantages of incorporation of CQDs and CdS QDs into the photoactive layer of ternary OSCs, it is interesting to discover the combined effects of both additives on the photovoltaic performance of quaternary OSCs. In this work, CdS QDs were incorporated as the fourth component into the previously optimized photoactive layer of P3HT:PC61BM:CQDs. The PCE increased by 12 % due to improved light absorption, exciton dissociation and electron transport.

Fig. 1 depicts the current density-voltage (J−V) curves of the OSCs with and without CdS QDs obtained under simulated AM 1.5 G illumination of 100 mWcm−2. Table 1 summarizes the Jsc, Voc, FF, and PCE of the OSCs. The results represent an average of at least five individual devices. The OSC without CdS QDs exhibited a PCE of 2.58 %, Voc of 0.46 V, Jsc of 11.80 mA/cm2, and FF of 0.47, which is in agreement with the photovoltaic parameters of the reported P3HT: PC61BM-based OSCs [14]. The OSC with CdS QDs demonstrated a higher PCE of 2.87 %, with Voc of 0.48 V, Jsc of 12.60 mA/cm2, and FF of 0.48. All the photovoltaic parameters increased by incorporating the CdS QDs into the photoactive layer, leading to 12 % enhancement in PCE compared to the OSC without CdS QDs. Although the PCE of 2.87 % is still low compared to the value reported for CdS-incorporated P3HT:PC61BM based OSCs [16], more satisfactory results could be obtained when a hole transport layer is inserted between anode and photoactive layer.

The photoactive layers were subjected to several characterizations to determine the roles of CdS QDs in improving the various photovoltaic parameters of OSCs. FESEM cross-sectional images of photoactive layers deposited on ZnO nanorod arrays are shown in Fig. 2 . In the case of samples without CdS QDs, the thickness of the photoactive layer on top of the ZnO nanorod arrays layer was determined to be 255 ± 21 nm. Meanwhile, the photoactive layer with CdS QDs had a slightly higher thickness of 266 ± 15 nm. It is believed that the increased thickness is partly due to the additional amount of CdS QDs in the photoactive layer solution. In general, a more concentrated solution results in the formation of a thicker layer prepared by the spin coating technique [22–24].

Fig. 3 a shows the UV–Vis absorption spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs. It is worth mentioning that the CdS QDs intrinsic absorption (in the range of 400–500 nm, as seen in Fig. 3c) did not contribute to the overall absorption of the photoactive layer due to the tiny amount of CdS QDs added. The photoactive layer with CdS QDs shows a small absorption increment in the 500–580 nm region compared to that without CdS QDs. As supported by the FESEM analysis, higher light absorption can be attributed to the thicker CdS QDs-incorporated photoactive layer. A higher generation of exciton because of photons interacting with a larger volume of material contributes to increased Jsc values. The normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs are displayed in Fig. 3b. After incorporating CdS QDs, the intensity of absorption shoulder at ∼610 nm was found to be lower, indicating the crystallinity of P3HT has decreased because the order of P3HT chains has been altered by CdS QDs.

Fig. 4 shows the Raman spectra of photoactive layers with and without CdS QDs. The degree of crystallinity within the P3HT chain can be related to the intensity ratio of CC bond to C–C intra-ring (IC
C/IC-C). The IC
C/IC-C of photoactive layer without CdS QDs was estimated to be 4.68. IC
C/IC-C increased to 5.27 after adding CdS QDs to the photoactive layer. It is easy to perceive that the higher IC
C/IC-C correlates with reduced planarity and crystallinity within the P3HT chain [25]. This finding is consistent with the normalized UV–Vis absorption spectra results in Fig. 3b. Even though the light absorption can be enhanced by the incorporation of CdS QDs, the hole transport across the photoactive layer could be disturbed by the CdS QDs. It is expected that hole transport pathways in the P3HT region were affected due to the decreased ordering of P3HT chains.

Fig. 5 a shows the PL spectra of photoactive layer with and without CdS QDs. The PL intensity decreased significantly when the CdS QDs were incorporated in the photoactive layer, indicating a better exciton dissociation efficiency. As illustrated in Fig. 5b, the conduction band of CdS QDs is lower than the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level of P3HT [26]. In addition to P3HT/PC61BM and P3HT/CQDs interfaces, the exciton generated in P3HT can also be dissociated at the CdS/P3HT interface. An additional pathway for exciton dissociation leads to a more effective charge transfer from P3HT to CdS. Similar to the previous report, a more effective exciton dissociation reduces exciton recombination, hence resulting in a higher Jsc and improved PCE [27].

To achieve a high FF, it is desirable to have a small series resistance (Rs) and a large shunt resistance (Rsh). The Rs decreased from 14.8 to 12.5 Ωcm2, whereas the Rsh increased from 165 to 254 Ωcm2 with the introduction of CdS QDs (Table 1). The conduction band of CdS QDs also matches the LUMO levels of PC61BM and CQDs, resulting in an effective cascade energy level alignment. The free electrons generated from the exciton dissociation at the P3HT/PC61BM and P3HT/CQDs interfaces can be transported efficiently to the ZnO electron transport layer via the CdS QDs. Lower Rs and higher Rsh for CdS QDs-incorporated OSCs indicate efficient electron extraction and reduced charge recombination [28,29].

Fig. 6 presents the IPCE spectra of OSCs with and without CdS QDs. The highest IPCE of the OSC without CdS QDs reached only ∼67 %, while OSC with CdS QDs managed to achieve ∼71 %. The Jsc values obtained from IPCE measurement, Jsc = 11.63 mA/cm2 for the OSC without CdS QDs, and Jsc = 12.95 mA/cm2 for the OSC with CdS QDs. These values agree with the Jsc values obtained from the J-V curve. It is important to point out that there is no significant change of the spectrum shape in the region of 400–500 nm, where the prominent CdS absorption peak is located in, suggesting the direct photon conversion to charge carriers by CdS can be neglected. This finding agrees well with the UV–Vis absorption spectra results in Fig. 3a, in which the absorption by the CdS QDs can be ignored in relative to those of other components in photoactive layer.

The variation of OSCs photovoltaic parameters as a function of CdS QDs concentrations is given in Table 2 . The photovoltaic performance was found to be greatly dependent on the amount of CdS QDs in the photoactive layer. The highest PCE of 2.87 % was recorded at 4 wt% of CdS QDs. However, when the concentration of CdS QDs exceeded the optimum amount of 4 wt%, the PCE started to decrease significantly, reaching a value of 1.75 % at 16 wt% CdS QDs. It is strongly believed that the addition of excess CdS QDs (more than 4 wt%) may result in agglomeration, which would prevent the P3HT donor and the dominant PCBM acceptor from mixing evenly. Consequently, the exciton dissociation efficiency and the charge transportation reduce, hence leading to a significant decrement in Jsc and PCE.

The effects of incorporating CdS QDs into the photoactive layer on the photovoltaic performance of quaternary OSCs have been investigated. It is interesting to find that the Jsc, Voc, FF and PCE all improved with the incorporation of CdS QDs. The presence of CdS QDs leads to higher light absorption, more effective exciton dissociation and electron transport. The OSC with CdS QDs concentration of 4 wt%. demonstrated the highest PCE of 2.87 %, an increment of 12 % compared to that without CdS QDs. This finding also proposes that the photovoltaic performance of ternary OSCs could be further enhanced by introducing the fourth component in the photoactive layer. It is believed that the same approach can be extended to other very high performing photoactive layers consisting of small band gap polymer donor and non-fullerene acceptor, which promotes the commercialization of OSCs in the future.

Nour Attallah Issa: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Sin Tee Tan: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Chin Hua Chia: Resources. Kam Sheng Lau: Investigation. Mohammad Hafizuddin Hj Jumali: Supervision, Resources. Kok-Keong Chong: Resources. Chi Chin Yap: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition.

CdS QDs were synthesized from cadmium and sulfur sources in an exchange reaction using a chemical bath deposition method [20]. Cadmium acetate dihydrate (Cd(CH3COO)2 · 2H2O, Sigma-Aldrich, purity 98 %) (0.05 M) was mixed with 3 ml of de-ionized water and stirred in ultrasonic for 5 min until the transparent solution formed. After 5 min, sodium sulfide hydrate (Na2S · xH2O, Sigma-Aldrich, purity ≥60 %) (0.05 M) was added and stirred ultrasonically for 5 min. The mixture was then shaken and stirred at 500 rpm for 10 min. Finally, it was treated hydrothermally at a temperature of 210 °C for 20 min. The resulting solution turned from transparent to orange colour. A diluted CdS QDs solution was prepared by mixing 9.9 mL of propanol and 0.1 mL of CdS QDs stock solution.

The OSCs were manufactured on glass substrates coated with fluorine tin oxide (FTO). The detailed procedures of preparing ZnO nanorod array electron transport layer, P3HT:PC61BM photoactive layers, and CQDs can be found in our previous studies [14,21]. 2 wt% of CQDs and 4 wt% of CdS QDs were added to P3HT:PC61BM photoactive layer solution. The photoactive layer solutions with and without CdS QDs were spin-coated onto the ZnO electron transport layer at 1150 rpm for 60 s. Then, the samples were heated at 120 °C for 10 min. The deposition of the photoactive layer was carried out in a glove box at a temperature of around 25 °C and a humidity level of approximately 15 %. To complete the OSC fabrication, a thermal evaporator was utilized to deposit a 100 nm silver (Ag) electrode onto the photoactive layer under a vacuum level of 5 × 10−5 mbar. The active area was 0.07 cm2.

The current-voltage (I–V) measurements were conducted using a Keithley 2401 source measurement unit to determine the photovoltaic characteristics of the OSCs, which include the Jsc, open-circuit voltage (Voc), fill factor (FF), and PCE. These measurements were conducted under the illumination of a Newport 96000 solar simulator at an intensity of 100 mW/cm2. Meanwhile, the incident photon to current conversion efficiency (IPCE) of the OSCs was measured using a Newport IPCE measurement system at a chopping frequency of 10 Hz.

For photoactive layer characterization, the thickness of the photoactive layers was analyzed by observing cross-sectional images using a Zeiss Merlin Compact field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM) at an accelerating voltage of 3 kV. The optical absorption spectra of the photoactive layers were characterized using a PerkinElmer Lambda 900 UV–Vis–NIR spectrophotometer in the 300–800 nm wavelength range. The photoluminescence (PL) properties of the photoactive layers were obtained using an Edinburg FLS920 fluorescence spectrometer at an excitation wavelength of 495 nm. Lastly, the crystallinity of P3HT was investigated using a Technospex Raman-Ci Raman micro-spectroscopy system equipped with a 532 nm laser and a Nikon Eclipse Ci L optical microscope.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Malaysian Ministry of Higher Education for providing financial support through Fundamental Research Grant Scheme (FRGS) under the grant number (FRGS/1/2020/STG07/UKM/02/4). The authors also acknowledge Centre for Research and Instrumentation Management (CRIM) Universiti Kebangsaan Malaysia for the analytical services given.
